title: Multiple Filters, Multiple Feature Maps
concept: A convolution layer doesn't use just one filter — it runs many filters in parallel over the same image, each learning to detect a different pattern like an edge or a corner, and each filter produces its own feature map, stacked together as the layer's output.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In the convolution operation, what are the two math steps applied to the filter numbers and the pixels beneath them?
exercises.explain: Right. Now let's see what happens when a layer runs many filters like this at once.

cards.title: One filter finds one thing
cards.body: A single learned filter is a specialist — it might become great at spotting vertical edges, but useless for spotting corners or color blobs. So what does a real CNN do? It doesn't rely on just one filter.

cards.title: A layer uses many filters
cards.body: A convolution layer runs many filters over the same image at once, often dozens of them. Each filter slides independently across the whole image, and each one specializes in detecting a different pattern.

cards.title: A team of specialist detectives
cards.body: Imagine a team of detectives examining the same photo: one only looks for straight edges, another only looks for sharp corners, another only looks for patches of red. Each detective writes up their own separate report — a convolution layer works the same way, one report per filter.

cards.title: Stacking the outputs
cards.body: If a layer has 16 filters, sliding each one across the image produces 16 separate outputs, called feature maps. One feature map might light up wherever there's a vertical edge; another might light up wherever there's a curve. Stacked together, they form the layer's full output.

cards.title: The filters aren't told what to look for
cards.body: It's easy to assume an engineer assigns each filter a job, like 'filter 3, find corners.' Nobody does that. Every filter starts random, and training simply nudges each one toward whatever pattern turns out to be useful, with no filter forced to specialize in anything specific.

cards.title: One filter vs. many filters
cards.body: Going from a single detector to a full team:

left.text:
One feature map
detects one kind of pattern

right.text:
A stack of feature maps
each detecting something different

exercises.prompt: Why does a convolution layer use many filters instead of just one?

options.text: Because each filter learns to detect a different pattern, building up a richer set of feature maps
Because one filter can only be applied to grayscale images
Because using multiple filters makes the image file smaller
Because a single filter can only slide across half the image

exercises.explain: Right — many specialist filters together capture far more patterns than one filter alone.

exercises.prompt: True or false: every filter in a convolution layer is manually assigned a specific pattern to detect, like 'this one finds corners.'
exercises.explain: False. Filters aren't assigned jobs — they self-organize into different specialists purely through training.

exercises.sentence: A convolution layer with 32 filters sliding over the same image produces 32 separate ___.
exercises.explain: Feature maps is right — one output map per filter, stacked together.

exercises.prompt: A convolution layer has 8 filters. Which statement correctly describes its output?

options.text: A stack of 8 feature maps, each showing where a different learned pattern was detected
A single feature map that blends the results of all 8 filters into one
8 separate copies of the original, unmodified image

exercises.explain: Right — each filter contributes its own independent feature map to the stack.

exercises.prompt: A CNN's first convolution layer has 64 filters. After training, filter #12 lights up strongly on diagonal edges, while filter #45 lights up on green color blobs. What best explains why these two filters ended up detecting completely different things?

options.text: Each filter starts with different random numbers, and backpropagation nudges each one independently, so they naturally specialize in different useful patterns
An engineer manually programmed filter #12 to find edges and filter #45 to find green blobs
All 64 filters actually produce identical output; the difference is just a display glitch
Filters are randomly reassigned a new job after every training step

exercises.explain: Exactly — independent random starts plus independent training let filters specialize on their own. Next: why this whole setup is so much more efficient than connecting every pixel to every neuron.
